value.content: Bias is inherently a societal issue with substantial public impact. Thus, focusing solely on technically improving an algorithm's prediction

value.title: [PDF] The Unintended Consequences of Algorithmic Bias
value.content: Lack of diversity in the programming field, the unconscious bias of data scientists, and the lack of representative datasets used to train systems contribute to the creation and deployment of machine learning software that perpetuate systemic inequities. For example, courts across the nation use risk assessments to guide data-driven Algorithmic bias occurs when algorithms systematically interpret data in a way that produces unintended discriminatory results. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has long held safeguards to protect consumers from algorithmic bias, such as Section 5 of the FTC Act, the Fair Credit Reporting Act, and the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and has recently published guidelines for businesses using machine learning technology and ADS. In partnership with the FTC, legislators must uniformly work to address the ways algorithmic bias can infringe on civil rights and create enhanced oversight and regulations to protect consumers from the rampant misuse of data in public and private systems.

value.title: New Brookings report highlights potential sources of bias in algorithms
value.content: The report outlines a number of instances of algorithmic bias, from recruiting tools that favor male job candidates based on prior hires to online ads.
